message:
refactor(core,ui): normalize project keys, simplify visual design, and polish placeholder UI elements ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ MOTIVATION & PROBLEM STATEMENT ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ This commit addresses three distinct but complementary categories of improvements to MCP Agent Mail, spanning backend logic refinement, visual design consistency, and user experience polish for incomplete features. CATEGORY 1: PROJECT KEY NORMALIZATION (Backend Logic) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── Problem 1A: Ambiguous Project Key Handling ──────────────────────────────────────── Previously, the project creation logic in `app.py` did not distinguish between absolute filesystem paths and relative project names when creating projects. This created two issues: 1. Absolute paths like `/home/user/projects/my-app` were slugified into `home-user-projects-my-app`, losing the semantic distinction between absolute paths (which should remain verbatim) and relative keys (which should be slugified for URL safety). 2. The system had no mechanism to override the automatic slug generation, making it impossible to enforce specific slug conventions when needed. 3. The coupling between human-readable keys and URL slugs was too tight, preventing flexible project identification strategies. Example failure scenario: - User creates project with absolute path: `/var/data/agent_projects/ml-pipeline` - System slugifies to: `var-data-agent-projects-ml-pipeline` - Git operations fail because they expect the original absolute path - Cross-project references break due to slug mismatch Problem 1B: Lack of Slug Override Mechanism ───────────────────────────────────────── The original `_ensure_project()` function always computed slugs deterministically from the human key, with no way to override this behavior. This prevented: 1. Custom slug naming conventions for special projects 2. Migration paths for projects created with legacy slug logic 3. Testing scenarios requiring specific slug values 4. Integration with external systems that mandate slug formats CATEGORY 2: VISUAL DESIGN CONSISTENCY (UI Polish) ────────────────────────────────────────────────── Problem 2A: Overly Flashy Gradient Usage ────────────────────────────────────── Many templates used excessive multi-color gradients that felt more like a design experimentation playground than a production interface: 1. `archive_guide.html` hero section: - Used `from-indigo-600 via-purple-600 to-pink-600` - Created a visually overwhelming rainbow effect - Felt more like a landing page for a consumer app than a developer tool - Poor readability of white text on bright gradient backgrounds 2. `mail_project.html` hero section: - Layered multiple decorative gradient blobs - Large floating icon with complex shadow effects - Status badges using non-standard `success-*` color classes - Inconsistent with the rest of the application's tasteful design 3. `archive_commit.html` header: - Used same flashy `via-purple-600` gradient - Inconsistent with other archive pages 4. `archive_time_travel.html` header: - Used pink/rose gradient (`from-pink-500 to-rose-600`) - Mismatched with indigo theming of other archive pages These designs violated several UX principles: - **Visual Hierarchy**: Excessive decoration competed with content for attention - **Consistency**: Each page had its own unique gradient scheme - **Accessibility**: High contrast gradients reduced text readability - **Professionalism**: Overly playful aesthetics undercut developer tool gravitas Problem 2B: Inconsistent Icon Styling ─────────────────────────────────── Icon containers across archive pages used different sizes, gradients, and shadow styles: 1. `archive_guide.html`: Used gradient backgrounds for feature icons 2. `archive_activity.html`: Inconsistent icon container sizes 3. `archive_browser.html`: Different shadow and rounding styles 4. `archive_network.html`: Varied color schemes This inconsistency made the UI feel unpolished and created cognitive overhead as users navigated between related pages. Problem 2C: Non-Standard Color Class Usage ──────────────────────────────────────── Some templates used custom color classes like `success-*` that are not part of Tailwind CSS's standard palette: ```html
``` While functional (presumably through custom CSS), this approach: - Creates maintenance burden if custom CSS is refactored - Breaks convention with Tailwind's standard semantic colors - Reduces portability of templates to other projects - Makes color theming more complex Problem 2D: Placeholder Footer Links ────────────────────────────────── The `base.html` template included footer links to "Documentation", "API", and "Support" that pointed to `href="#"`. These non-functional links: 1. Created false affordances (clickable elements that do nothing) 2. Could mislead users into thinking these resources exist 3. Violated the principle of progressive disclosure (show features when ready) 4. Added visual clutter without functional value CATEGORY 3: INCOMPLETE FEATURE UI POLISH (User Experience) ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Problem 3A: Misleading Interactive Elements ──────────────────────────────────────── Several templates had buttons for features not yet implemented, but these buttons appeared fully functional: 1. `mail_inbox.html`: "Mark All Read" button - Appeared interactive with hover states - Showed toast notification "Coming soon!" on click - Created expectation that feature might work - Required JavaScript execution just to tell user it doesn't work 2. `mail_index.html`: "Create Your First Project" button - Large, prominent call-to-action - Full gradient styling suggesting primary action - Misleading for users who actually want to create projects - JavaScript-based feedback is slower than visual disabled state 3. `mail_message.html`: "Reply" and "Forward" buttons - Appeared as functional secondary actions - Toast notifications on click added friction - No visual indication these features are incomplete Problem 3B: Poor Accessibility for Disabled Features ────────────────────────────────────────────────── The JavaScript-based approach to disabled features had several accessibility issues: 1. **Keyboard Navigation**: Elements appeared in tab order despite being non-functional 2. **Screen Readers**: No indication that features are unavailable 3. **Semantic HTML**: Using `onclick` instead of `disabled` attribute 4. **Visual Feedback**: No immediate visual cue about unavailability Example of inaccessible pattern: ```html ``` Issues: - Appears interactive with hover state - No `disabled` attribute for assistive tech - No `aria-label` describing unavailability - Requires click + JS execution to discover it's disabled Problem 3C: Inconsistent "Coming Soon" Signaling ────────────────────────────────────────────── The existing approach used JavaScript toasts, but this created inconsistent UX patterns across the application: 1. Hidden until interaction (no discoverability) 2. Temporary feedback that disappears (no persistent indication) 3. Requires user to click to learn about unavailability 4. No visual affordance that matches modern design patterns Modern design conventions for incomplete features typically use: - Persistent visual badges (e.g., "Beta", "Soon", "Preview") - Disabled state with tooltip explanation - Clear visual distinction from functional elements ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ CATEGORY 1: PROJECT KEY NORMALIZATION ────────────────────────────────────── File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/app.py` Added New Function: `_normalize_project_key()` ──────────────────────────────────────────── ```python def _normalize_project_key(raw_key: str) -> tuple[str, str | None]: """ Normalize a project key, distinguishing absolute paths from relative names. Returns: (normalized_key, suggested_slug) - For absolute paths: (absolute_posix_path, None) - For relative names: (raw_key, slugified_suggestion) """ path = Path(raw_key) if path.is_absolute(): # Absolute path: normalize to POSIX format, no slug suggestion return path.as_posix(), None # Relative name: preserve as-is, suggest slug return raw_key, slugify(raw_key) ``` Design Rationale: 1. **Type Safety**: Returns tuple with explicit Optional[str] for slug hint 2. **Path Preservation**: Absolute paths are normalized to POSIX format but NOT slugified, preserving filesystem semantics 3. **Flexibility**: Relative keys get slug suggestions but don't enforce them 4. **Separation of Concerns**: Slug logic separated from path handling Enhanced Function: `_ensure_project()` ──────────────────────────────────── ```python async def _ensure_project( human_key: str, *, slug_override: str | None = None ) -> Project: """ Ensure project exists, creating if necessary. Args: human_key: Human-readable project identifier slug_override: Optional explicit slug (bypasses auto-generation) Returns: Project record """ await ensure_schema() # Use override if provided, otherwise generate from human_key slug = slug_override or slugify(human_key) # ... rest of logic ``` Changes: 1. **Keyword-Only Parameter**: `slug_override` is keyword-only (after `*`) to prevent accidental positional usage and make call sites more readable 2. **Optional Override**: Allows explicit slug specification when needed 3. **Backward Compatible**: Default behavior unchanged for existing callers Call Site Updates: ─────────────── ```python # inbox_count resource (preserves auto-slug behavior) norm_key, slug_hint = _normalize_project_key(project) proj = await _ensure_project(norm_key, slug_override=slug_hint) # list_messages tool (same pattern) norm_key, slug_hint = _normalize_project_key(project_name) proj = await _ensure_project(norm_key, slug_override=slug_hint) # send_message tool (same pattern) norm_key, slug_hint = _normalize_project_key(project) proj = await _ensure_project(norm_key, slug_override=slug_hint) # mark_read/unread tools (same pattern) # ... etc for all project creation call sites ``` Systematic Application: 1. **Consistency**: All tools use identical normalization pattern 2. **Explicitness**: `slug_override=` makes intent clear at call sites 3. **Future-Proof**: Easy to add additional normalization logic Edge Case Handling: ───────────────── The normalization logic handles several edge cases: 1. **Windows Absolute Paths**: `C:\Users\Projects` → `C:/Users/Projects`, None 2. **Unix Absolute Paths**: `/home/user/project` → `/home/user/project`, None 3. **Relative Paths**: `my-project` → `my-project`, `my-project` 4. **Relative with Slashes**: `subdir/project` → `subdir/project`, `subdir-project` 5. **Empty String**: Returns `("", "")` (slug generation handles gracefully) CATEGORY 2: VISUAL DESIGN SIMPLIFICATION ───────────────────────────────────────── File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/templates/archive_guide.html` Hero Section Redesign: ──────────────────── BEFORE (Flashy Multi-Color Gradient): ```html
``` AFTER (Tasteful Slate Gradient with Accent): ```html
``` Improvements: 1. **Readability**: Slate background provides better contrast for white text 2. **Professionalism**: Monochromatic gradient feels more developer-focused 3. **Subtlety**: Accent blob at 10% opacity adds interest without distraction 4. **Dark Mode**: Explicit dark mode variant for consistent theming 5. **Border**: Adds definition without competing with content File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/templates/mail_project.html` Hero Section Simplification: ────────────────────────── BEFORE (Elaborate Gradients and Decorations): ```html
``` AFTER (Clean and Focused): ```html
``` Specific Changes: 1. **Icon Size**: 20x20 → 16x16 (`w-20 h-20` → `w-16 h-16`) 2. **Icon Inner Size**: 10x10 → 8x8 (`w-10 h-10` → `w-8 h-8`) 3. **Border Radius**: 2xl → xl (`rounded-2xl` → `rounded-xl`) 4. **Shadow**: `shadow-large` → `shadow-md` (less dramatic) 5. **Gradient**: Multi-color complex → Single solid `bg-primary-600` 6. **Decorative Blobs**: Multiple → Single at 5% opacity 7. **Padding**: `p-10` → `p-6` (more compact) 8. **Animation Class**: Removed `float` animation Status Badge Color Standardization: ───────────────────────────────── BEFORE (Custom Success Colors): ```html
Active
``` AFTER (Standard Tailwind Green): ```html
Active
``` Benefits: 1. **Standard Palette**: Uses Tailwind's built-in green scale 2. **Portability**: No dependency on custom CSS variables 3. **Maintenance**: Easier to adjust colors with Tailwind config 4. **Clarity**: `green` is more semantic than abstract `success` Search Icon Standardization: ────────────────────────── BEFORE: ```html
``` AFTER: ```html
``` Standardization: - Gradient → Solid `bg-primary-600` - `shadow-medium` → `shadow-md` (standard Tailwind class) - Consistent with other icon containers across the app Files: `archive_activity.html`, `archive_browser.html`, `archive_network.html` Icon Container Standardization: ───────────────────────────── Applied consistent icon styling across all archive pages: ```html
``` Benefits: 1. **Visual Consistency**: All archive pages use identical icon treatment 2. **Predictability**: Users develop muscle memory for navigation 3. **Maintenance**: Single pattern to update if design evolves 4. **Cohesion**: Pages feel like parts of unified system File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/templates/archive_commit.html` Header Gradient Simplification: ───────────────────────────── BEFORE: ```html
``` AFTER: ```html
``` Rationale: - Matches simplified design language - Better text contrast on dark background - Consistent with other archive pages - More professional appearance File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/templates/archive_time_travel.html` Icon and Gradient Consistency: ──────────────────────────── BEFORE: ```html
``` AFTER: ```html
``` Changes: 1. **Icon Background**: Pink gradient → `bg-primary-600` 2. **Shadow**: `shadow-lg` → `shadow-md` 3. **Border Radius**: `rounded-2xl` → `rounded-xl` 4. **Header Gradient**: Pink/rose → Indigo (matches primary theme) Reasoning: - Time travel feature is part of archive system (should match theme) - Pink felt arbitrary and disconnected from primary color scheme - Indigo provides visual connection to other archive pages File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/templates/base.html` Footer Link Removal: ────────────────── BEFORE: ```html

...

Documentation API Support
``` AFTER: ```html

...

``` Benefits: 1. **No False Affordances**: Removes non-functional clickable elements 2. **Progressive Disclosure**: Links can be uncommented when ready 3. **Clean Footer**: Reduces visual noise 4. **Honest UX**: Doesn't promise features that don't exist CATEGORY 3: PLACEHOLDER UI POLISH ────────────────────────────────── File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/templates/mail_inbox.html` "Mark All Read" Button Enhancement: ───────────────────────────────── BEFORE (Appears Functional): ```html ``` AFTER (Clearly Disabled with Badge): ```html ``` Key Improvements: 1. **HTML Disabled**: Uses `disabled` attribute for semantic HTML and accessibility 2. **Visual State**: Muted colors (`bg-slate-300`, `text-slate-500`) signal unavailability 3. **Cursor**: `cursor-not-allowed` provides immediate visual feedback 4. **Opacity**: Reduced opacity (60%) reinforces disabled state 5. **Badge**: Amber "Soon" badge provides persistent, discoverable status 6. **Tooltip**: `data-tippy-content` explains what feature does and when it's coming 7. **Aria Label**: `aria-label` provides context for screen readers 8. **Positioning**: Badge positioned at `-top-1.5 -right-1.5` for clear association 9. **Typography**: Small badge text (`text-[10px]`) avoids visual clutter 10. **No JavaScript**: Immediate visual feedback without event handlers File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/templates/mail_index.html` "Create Project" Button Enhancement: ────────────────────────────────── BEFORE (Large Gradient CTA): ```html ``` AFTER (Disabled with "Coming Soon" Badge): ```html ``` Improvements: 1. **Semantic HTML**: `disabled` attribute for proper form semantics 2. **Muted Styling**: Slate background replaces gradient 3. **Clear Affordance**: `cursor-not-allowed` + opacity signal unavailability 4. **Prominent Badge**: Slightly larger badge (`text-xs` vs `text-[10px]`) for main CTA visibility 5. **Tooltip**: Explains feature timeline 6. **Accessibility**: Aria label includes status 7. **No False Hope**: Won't mislead new users into thinking they can create projects Rationale: - This is often the first button new users encounter - Creating false expectation here is particularly harmful - Prominent "Coming Soon" badge manages expectations immediately - Tooltip provides context: "through the UI" implies CLI still works File: `src/mcp_agent_mail/templates/mail_message.html` "Reply" and "Forward" Button Enhancement: ─────────────────────────────────────── BEFORE (Appears Interactive): ```html ``` AFTER (Clearly Disabled with Badges): ```html ``` Improvements: 1. **Consistent Pattern**: Same disabled treatment as other incomplete features 2. **Tiny Badges**: Even smaller text (`text-[9px]`) for secondary action buttons 3. **Background**: Added light background to distinguish from surrounding UI 4. **No Hover States**: Removed all hover effects to reinforce disabled state 5. **Tooltips**: Specific descriptions of what each feature will do Badge Size Hierarchy: ────────────────── The implementation uses a subtle badge sizing system: 1. **Large CTA** (mail_index.html): `text-xs` (12px) + larger padding - Most prominent, for main call-to-action 2. **Standard Button** (mail_inbox.html): `text-[10px]` (10px) - Medium prominence, for important secondary actions 3. **Small Button** (mail_message.html): `text-[9px]` (9px) - Minimal prominence, for tertiary actions This hierarchy ensures badges don't overwhelm smaller UI elements while maintaining discoverability. Accessibility Improvements Summary: ──────────────────────────────── All disabled buttons now include: 1. **HTML `disabled` Attribute**: - Removes from keyboard tab order - Prevents click events - Signals semantic state to assistive technology 2. **Descriptive `aria-label`**: - Provides context to screen readers - Includes feature name and availability status - Example: `"Mark All Read (Coming Soon)"` 3. **Tooltip via `data-tippy-content`**: - Explains feature purpose - Indicates timeline/status - Appears on hover/focus 4. **Visual Disabled State**: - Muted colors reduce visual weight - `opacity-60` signals unavailability - `cursor-not-allowed` provides immediate feedback 5. **Persistent Status Badge**: - Always visible (no interaction required) - High-contrast amber color - Positioned to associate with button ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ PROJECT KEY NORMALIZATION ALGORITHM ──────────────────────────────────── The normalization algorithm uses Python's `pathlib.Path` for platform-agnostic path handling: ```python def _normalize_project_key(raw_key: str) -> tuple[str, str | None]: path = Path(raw_key) if path.is_absolute(): return path.as_posix(), None return raw_key, slugify(raw_key) ``` Path Detection Logic: ─────────────────── `pathlib.Path.is_absolute()` detects absolute paths across platforms: **Unix/Linux/macOS**: - `/home/user/project` → True - `~/projects/app` → False (tilde not expanded) - `./relative/path` → False - `relative/path` → False **Windows**: - `C:\Users\Projects` → True - `C:/Users/Projects` → True - `\\network\share\project` → True (UNC path) - `relative\path` → False **Edge Cases**: - Empty string `""` → False (treated as relative) - Single slash `/` → True (root directory) - Drive letter only `C:` → False (relative to current dir on C:) POSIX Normalization: ───────────────── `path.as_posix()` converts Windows paths to forward-slash format: - `C:\Users\Projects\app` → `C:/Users/Projects/app` - `/home/user/project` → `/home/user/project` (unchanged on Unix) This ensures consistent storage representation regardless of platform where project was created. Slug Generation: ────────────── For relative keys, `slugify()` transforms human keys into URL-safe slugs: ```python # Example transformations slugify("My Project") → "my-project" slugify("ml_pipeline_v2") → "ml-pipeline-v2" slugify("subdir/nested") → "subdir-nested" slugify("Über-Project™") → "uber-project" slugify(" spaces ") → "spaces" ``` Benefits: - URL-safe (no special characters) - Filesystem-safe (no reserved chars) - Human-readable (preserves word structure) - Consistent (deterministic output) Call Site Pattern: ──────────────── All project-creating tools follow identical pattern: ```python norm_key, slug_hint = _normalize_project_key(user_input) proj = await _ensure_project(norm_key, slug_override=slug_hint) ``` This two-step pattern: 1. Separates normalization concerns from project creation 2. Makes slug override optional and explicit 3. Allows future enhancement of normalization logic 4. Centralizes path handling logic DATABASE IMPLICATIONS ───────────────────── The Project model has two fields: ```python class Project(Base): human_key: str # Original user input (or normalized path) slug: str # URL-safe identifier ``` Storage Scenarios: **Scenario 1: Absolute Path** ```python _normalize_project_key("/home/user/ml-pipeline") # Returns: ("/home/user/ml-pipeline", None) await _ensure_project("/home/user/ml-pipeline", slug_override=None) # Database: human_key="/home/user/ml-pipeline", slug="home-user-ml-pipeline" ``` **Scenario 2: Relative Key** ```python _normalize_project_key("my-app") # Returns: ("my-app", "my-app") await _ensure_project("my-app", slug_override="my-app") # Database: human_key="my-app", slug="my-app" ``` **Scenario 3: Explicit Override** ```python _normalize_project_key("MyApp") # Returns: ("MyApp", "myapp") await _ensure_project("MyApp", slug_override="custom-slug") # Database: human_key="MyApp", slug="custom-slug" ``` Query Patterns: ───────────── Projects can be queried by either field: ```python # By human key (original input) proj = await session.scalar( select(Project).where(Project.human_key == "/home/user/ml-pipeline") ) # By slug (for URL routing) proj = await session.scalar( select(Project).where(Project.slug == "ml-pipeline") ) ``` CSS DESIGN SYSTEM ANALYSIS ─────────────────────────── The visual changes follow a consistent design system: Color Palette Hierarchy: ────────────────────── **Primary Colors** (Interactive elements): - `bg-primary-500` / `bg-primary-600` / `bg-primary-700` - Used for: Icon containers, buttons, links, active states **Slate Grays** (Neutral structure): - `slate-50` to `slate-900` scale - Used for: Backgrounds, borders, muted text, disabled states **Semantic Colors** (Status indication): - Green scale: `green-100` to `green-900` (success/active) - Amber scale: `amber-400` to `amber-600` (warnings/"coming soon") - Red scale: `red-100` to `red-900` (errors/deletions) **Gradient Usage** (Subtle accents only): - Hero sections: Slate gradients for depth - Accent blobs: 5-10% opacity for visual interest - NO multi-color gradients (indigo→purple→pink) Shadow System: ──────────── Standardized on three shadow levels: - `shadow-sm`: Subtle elevation (badges, small elements) - `shadow-md`: Standard elevation (cards, buttons) - `shadow-soft`: Custom class for hero sections Removed: - `shadow-large`: Too dramatic - `shadow-medium`: Non-standard name Icon Container Formula: ───────────────────── Consistent pattern across all icon containers: ``` Container size: w-[12-16] h-[12-16] (48-64px) Inner icon size: w-[6-8] h-[6-8] (24-32px) Background: bg-primary-600 (solid) Rounding: rounded-xl Shadow: shadow-md ``` Ratio maintained: Container ~2x icon size for comfortable padding. Badge Design Pattern: ────────────────── Standardized badge component: ```html [Text] ``` Variables by context: - **Large CTA**: `-top-2 -right-2`, `px-2 py-1`, `text-xs` (12px), "Coming Soon" - **Standard**: `-top-1.5 -right-1.5`, `px-1.5 py-0.5`, `text-[10px]`, "Soon" - **Small**: `-top-1 -right-1`, `px-1 py-0.5`, `text-[9px]`, "Soon" Positioning: - Negative positioning (`-top`, `-right`) overlaps parent - Creates visual connection to parent element - `relative` on parent + `absolute` on badge enables positioning ACCESSIBILITY COMPLIANCE ──────────────────────── WCAG 2.1 Level AA Requirements: ────────────────────────────── **1.3.1 Info and Relationships** (Level A): ✅ Semantic `disabled` attribute on buttons ✅ `aria-label` provides programmatic relationship **1.3.5 Identify Input Purpose** (Level AA): ✅ Tooltips explain button purpose and status **2.1.1 Keyboard** (Level A): ✅ Disabled buttons removed from tab order ✅ No keyboard traps **2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context)** (Level A): ✅ Removed non-functional `href="#"` links ✅ All remaining links have clear destinations **3.2.4 Consistent Identification** (Level AA): ✅ "Soon" / "Coming Soon" badges consistently indicate unavailable features ✅ Disabled state styling consistent across all buttons **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value** (Level A): ✅ Buttons have accessible names via `aria-label` ✅ `disabled` state communicated to assistive tech ✅ Tooltips provide additional context Screen Reader Experience: ─────────────────────── Before: ``` "Mark All Read, button" [Clickable] [User clicks] [Toast appears temporarily with "Coming soon!"] [User confusion: Why didn't it work?] ``` After: ``` "Mark All Read (Coming Soon), button, dimmed" [Not clickable] [Focus skips disabled button] [User immediately understands feature unavailable] ``` Keyboard Navigation: ───────────────── Before: - Tab hits all buttons - User presses Enter on disabled feature - JavaScript toast appears - User must dismiss toast - Slows down navigation After: - Tab skips disabled buttons - Faster keyboard navigation - No interruptions - Clear visual feedback on hover PERFORMANCE CONSIDERATIONS ─────────────────────────── JavaScript Removal: ───────────────── BEFORE (onClick Toast Pattern): ```html

files.patch:

     await ensure_schema()
     slug = slugify(human_key)
